coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-driven N and Fe co-doped carbon dots for peroxymonosulfate activation and highly efficient aminopyrine photodegradation
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qiansheng
Lu, Hong
Wang, Xiaolei
Hong, Zhongqiang
Fu, Ze
Liu, Xinxin
Zhou, Jiti
coredata.dc:description: Low solar energy utilization, instability of Fe ions and Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle difficulty are three main factors, limiting the photocatalytic activity of the Fe/peroxymonosulfate (PMS) system. Herein, based on the N and Fe co-doped carbon dots (N, Fe-CDs) synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method, a new Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle system for PMS activation under visible light irradiation was proposed. The characterization experiments showed that Fe has been successfully doped to the sp2 graphitic structure and mainly existed as Fe-N and Fe-OH complexes, which played an important role in harvesting visible light, enhancing the stability of Fe ions and acting as active sites to accelerate electron transfer. Under visible light irradiation, the photogenerated electrons of N, Fe-CDs could be directly transferred to Fe (III) for Fe (II) formation via the ligand-to-mental charge transfer process, thus accelerating the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle. When N, Fe-CDs were used for activating PMS under visible light irradiation, aminopyrine could be efficiently degraded and mineralized to CO2 via the cleavage of pyrazole ring, demethylation, hydroxylation, decarbonylation and the cleavage of benzene ring. The produced O2 − and 1O2 played the main roles in the whole photocatalytic reaction. The above results revealed that the novel N, Fe-CDs could effectively activate PMS under visible light irradiation for the degradation of pharmaceuticals in the polluted water.

The high-resolution XPS spectra of N-CDs and N, Fe-CDs. (a) C 1s; (b) N 1s; (c) O 1s; (d) Fe 2p.

The aminopyrine photodegradation efficiencies of N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system under different conditions. (a) pH (20 °C, 0.10 g/L N, Fe-CDs, 0.10 mM PMS, 0.10 mM aminopyrine); (b) N, Fe-CDs dosage (20 °C, pH 4, 0.10 mM PMS, 0.10 mM aminopyrine); (c) PMS concentration (20 °C, pH 4, 0.10 g/L N, Fe-CDs, 0.10 mM aminopyrine); (d) Aminopyrine concentration (20 °C, pH 4, 0.10 g/L N, Fe-CDs, 0.10 mM PMS).

The photocatalytic activities and stability of N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system. (a) Photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of aminopyrine over different catalysts; (b) The recycling tests of N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system; (c) The aminopyrine mineralization ratio of N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system detected by TOC analyzer; (d) The photogenerated Fe2+ of N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system in the presence or absence of PMS. Conditions: 20 °C, pH 4 (natural), 0.10 g/L N, Fe-CDs, 0.10 mM PMS, 0.10 mM aminopyrine.
Proposed photodegradation pathways of aminopyrine in N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system.

The detection of active species in N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system. (a) ROS scavenging experiments using different scavengers (0.5 M TBA for OH, 1.2 M MeOH for OH and SO4
−, 5 mM L-histidine for 1O2, 2 mM BQ for O2 −); (b) DMPO-trapped ESR spectra of OH and SO4
− (25 mM DMPO); (c) DMPO-trapped ESR spectra of O2 − (25 mM DMPO); (d) TEMP-trapped ESR spectra of 1O2 (25 mM TEMP).
The photocatalytic mechanism of N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system on aminopyrine.

head.ce:title: Visible-light-driven N and Fe co-doped carbon dots for peroxymonosulfate activation and highly efficient aminopyrine photodegradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering (Ministry of Education), School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering (Ministry of Education)
School of Environmental Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering (Ministry of Education), School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Low solar energy utilization, instability of Fe ions and Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle difficulty are three main factors, limiting the photocatalytic activity of the Fe/peroxymonosulfate (PMS) system. Herein, based on the N and Fe co-doped carbon dots (N, Fe-CDs) synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method, a new Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle system for PMS activation under visible light irradiation was proposed. The characterization experiments showed that Fe has been successfully doped to the sp2 graphitic structure and mainly existed as Fe-N and Fe-OH complexes, which played an important role in harvesting visible light, enhancing the stability of Fe ions and acting as active sites to accelerate electron transfer. Under visible light irradiation, the photogenerated electrons of N, Fe-CDs could be directly transferred to Fe (III) for Fe (II) formation via the ligand-to-mental charge transfer process, thus accelerating the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle. When N, Fe-CDs were used for activating PMS under visible light irradiation, aminopyrine could be efficiently degraded and mineralized to CO2 via the cleavage of pyrazole ring, demethylation, hydroxylation, decarbonylation and the cleavage of benzene ring. The produced O2 − and 1O2 played the main roles in the whole photocatalytic reaction. The above results revealed that the novel N, Fe-CDs could effectively activate PMS under visible light irradiation for the degradation of pharmaceuticals in the polluted water.

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) including Fenton, Fenton-like photocatalytic and ozonation have shown great potentials in environmental remediation [1,2]. Among them, the PMS-based AOPs have more advantages due to their stability, easy operation, economy and efficiency, and have received significant attention in the field of persistent pollutants elimination [3–5]. Since PMS itself has a low oxidation potential and cannot effectively degrade pollutants, further activation of PMS is required to produce more reactive oxygen species (ROS) [5]. Common activation processes including heating, microwave, UV/ionizing radiation and electrochemistry often require high input energy, limiting their application [4,6]. Activation methods based on transition metal ions (Co2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, etc) have also been widely investigated in PMS activation due to their simplicity, efficiency and economy [3,4]. Compared with other metal ions, the Fe ion is more popular for its environmental friendliness and cost-effectiveness [7,8]. The Fe-based activators with a variable chemical state and an unoccupied orbital can provide one electron to activate the -O-O- bond of PMS [7,9,10].

However, the current Fe/PMS catalytic system presents three distinct drawbacks: (i) The activation process often requires UV irradiation, yet UV light is only 3% of solar energy, and visible light, accounting for 44% of solar energy, is not effectively used [10–12]. (ii) The instability of Fe ions in solution. Their direct use may meet the issues of deposition and agglomeration sink [4,8,13], and excessive iron can lead to the accumulation of iron oxide sludge and secondary contamination [14]. (iii) The slow efficiency of the transformation from Fe(III) to Fe(II) is the rate-determining step of the Fe-based AOPs [15,16]. At present, there are several main categories to strengthen Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle (Table S1). The external stimulations such as UV, ultrasound and electrochemistry could accelerate the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle, but increased the cost [11,17,18]. It has been found that metal ion chelators including citrate, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), 3,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid (PCA) and ethylenediamine-N,N′-disuccinic acid (EDDS) can not only improve the stability of Fe ions in solution, but also accelerate the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle [11,19–21]. In addition, some reducing agents (polyphenols and hydroxylamines), and metal oxides or sulfides (MoO2 and MoS2) could significantly promote the conversion from Fe(III) to Fe(II) [7,15,22–24]. However, these reducing agents and organic ligands may also lead to secondary contamination, compete with target pollutants for ROS and increase the cost [14]. Metal oxides or sulfides existed the problems of heavy metal ions leaching [14].

Carbon dots (CDs, <10 nm) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials consisting of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms. CDs are of their unique physicochemical properties including unique optical properties, non-toxicity, chemical inertness, light stability, water solubility and easy surface modification [25–28]. Moreover, after photoexcitation, CDs can exhibit electron storage/transfer capabilities, implying that they have great potentials in photocatalytic applications [27,29–31]. The current studies have shown that heteroatom doping is an effective strategy to improve the photochemical performance of CDs [32–34]. Heteroatom doping can be divided into non-metallic and metallic elements doping [33,35]. It has been proved that N-doping can significantly improve the optical absorption performance of CDs, and N-CDs also exhibit enhanced electron storage/transfer capability [36,37]. Compared with non-metallic heteroatom doping, transition metal elements (like Fe) with variable valences and unoccupied orbitals can chelate with the functional groups of CDs and perform superior physicochemical properties like catalytic properties [33,38]. In comparison with single-atom doping, co-doping of multiple heteroatoms has received increasing attention because of their synergistic effects, which can form unique electronic structures in CDs [39]. To date, there are several reports of N, Fe-CDs, and they are mainly used as peroxidase and fluorescent probes for H2O2 determination, cell imaging and biomolecules determination like hematin, uric acid, xanthine and dopamine [40–44]. Little information was available about N, Fe-CDs as photocatalysts to accelerate Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle for PMS activation and contaminants degradation. Due to the low cost and environmentally friendly properties of CDs, it is necessary to design and investigate N and Fe co-doped CDs for environmental remediation.

Pyrazolone pharmaceutical like aminopyrine, as an antipyretic and analgesic drug, has been widely used since the 1990s. However, it could cause serious side effects like granulocytopenia and myelosuppression. Most aminopyrine might enter into surface water and threaten human health. The current treatment methods mainly use disinfectants including O3, Cl2, Cl2O and ClO2 [45–47]. At present, little information is available about aminopyrine degradation by using the N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis photocatalytic system.

In this work, the N, Fe-CDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal process, and a new Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle system for PMS activation based on N, Fe-CDs/vis was proposed. Their morphology, structure and physicochemical properties were analyzed. N, Fe-CDs were used as visible-light-driven PMS activators for aminopyrine degradation. The effects of pH, N, Fe-CDs dosage, PMS concentration, aminopyrine concentration and anions were also investigated in detail. Based on the analysis results of photodegradation products and ROS, the possible photodegradation pathways of aminopyrine and photocatalytic mechanism of N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system were proposed.

Diethylenetriamine (DETA, 99%) and ethyl acetate (99.9%) were purchased from Macklin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ferric trichloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 99%) Tert-butyl alcohol (TBA, 99%), citric acid anhydrous (CA, 99%) and sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate (Na2S2O3·5H2O, 99%) were purchased from Tianjin Damao chemicals Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). p-benzoquinone (BQ, 99%) and peroxymonosulfate (KHSO5·0.5KHSO4·0.5K2SO4, PMS) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Aminopyrine (98%, Rhawn, China). L-histidine (99%, Solarbio, China). Methanol (MeOH, 99.9%, Oceanpak, Sweden).

N, Fe-CDs were synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal process. Firstly, 6.2 mmol CA and 0.55 mmol FeCl3·6H2O were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water. Then, 1.4 mmol DETA was added to the above solution to form the precursor solution. The precursor solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 230 °C for 12 h. The product was further filtered (0.22 μm aqueous filter membranes) and dialyzed with a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000 Da) for 6 h after cooling down to room temperature. Finally, the brown powder was got through a freeze-dried method. N-CDs were prepared by the same process except for the absence of FeCl3·6H2O.

A transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA) and a high-resolution electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA) were used to analyze the morphology and structure of as-prepared samples. The chemical groups were recorded on a Fourier transforms infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, IRTracer-100, Shimadzu, Japan). Using a quartz substrate, the crystal phase was analyzed by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Ultimate IV, Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54056 Å) over the 2θ range from 5° to 80° at a scan rate of 4°/min, and the voltage and current were 40 kV and 30 mA, respectively. The surface chemical states were analyzed on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo, USA). ROS were analyzed by an electron spin resonance spectrometer (ESR, EMXplus, Bruker, Germany). A UV–vis spectrometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to analyze the optical absorption. A photoluminescence spectrometer (PL, FLS1000, Edinburgh, UK) was employed to investigate PL properties. An electrochemical workstation (versaSTAT3, Princeton, UK) was utilized to analyze photoelectrochemistry (PEC) properties.

The photocatalytic experiment was conducted under visible light irradiation, and the light source was simulated by a 150 W tungsten halogen lamp equipped with a light filter (≥400 nm). Typically, a 10 mL aqueous solution including 0.10 g/L N, Fe-CDs, 0.10 mM aminopyrine and 0.10 mM PMS was added to a 15 mL quartz reactor and magnetically stirred at 300 r/min. The reaction solution firstly reacted in dark for 30 min to achieve an adsorption–desorption equilibrium at room temperature (20 ± 1℃). 0.5 mL samples were firstly taken at every 15 min and 50 μL Na2S2O3 (100 mM) was added to quench the reaction. Then, the samples were filtered by 0.22 μm aqueous filter membranes and the concentration of aminopyrine was detected by a liquid chromatograph (LC).

In the above reaction system, active species trapping experiments were investigated by adding diverse scavengers before reaction such as 0.5 M TBA for OH, 1.2 M MeOH for OH and SO4
−, 5 mM L-histidine for 1O2, and 2 mM BQ for O2 −. All experiments were repeated 3 times under the same conditions to obtain the means and standard deviations (shown in Figures).

The concentration of aminopyrine was analyzed by the LC (20AT, Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with a Sapphire C18 column (4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm, Sepax Technologies, USA) and a UV detector (SPD-M20A, Shimadzu, Japan). The column was kept at 30 °C, and the mobile phase consisted of 50% methanol and 50% ultrapure water with a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. The injection volume was 10 μL and the detection wavelength of aminopyrine was 268 nm.

The analysis for the degradation products of aminopyrine was another system including 0.20 mM aminopyrine, 0.10 g/L N, Fe-CDs and 0.10 mM PMS. The samples were taken at 80 min without the addition of Na2S2O3 and filtered by 0.22 μm aqueous filter membranes. Then, the samples were investigated by a high-resolution liquid chromatograph-time of flight mass spectrometer (TOF LC-MS, G6224A, Agilent, USA) with an Ultimate XB-C18 column (2.1 × 100 mm, 5 μm, Welch Materials, USA). The mobile phases included ultrapure water (A) and methanol (B) at a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min. The gradient of mobile phases was as follows: 5% B in 0 min linearly increased to 10% in 5 min, then 10% B in 5 min linearly increased to 80% in 25 min and equilibrated for 15 min. The column temperature was at 30 °C and the injection volume was 10 μL. MS information was analyzed under the following conditions: electrospray ionization source (ESI+), capillary voltage (4000 V), mass scan range (100–700 m/z), gas flow rate (8 L/min) and gas temperature (350 °C).

For the small molecule products of aminopyrine, the samples (20 mL) were extracted 3 times with ethyl acetate (15 mL) and then concentrated to 1–2 mL under a mild N2 stream. Then, the concentrated solution was analyzed by using a gas chromatograph-mass spectrometer (GC–MS, 5975C, Agilent, USA). The analysis method of GC–MS was the same as a previous report [47].

Photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of CDs were investigated in a standard three-electrode system. Using nafion (5%) as membrane solution, the FTO glass coated by as-prepared samples (1.0 g/L) was used as the working electrode. A Pt plate and saturated calomel electrode (SCE, Hg/Hg2Cl2, saturated KCl) were set as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The electrolyte was supported by a 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. Under the open-circuit voltage, EIS was carried out in the frequency range of 0.01–100 kHz with a 10 mV amplitude of AC voltage. CDs (0.2 g/L) were prepared to investigated the optical properties including UV–vis absorption (200–800 nm) and PL (λex = 365 nm, λem = 445 nm).

The concentration of Fe2+ was investigated by the Ferronzine method [48]. Dark reaction and light reaction were used as the control group and experimental group, respectively. The photogenerated Fe2+ was calculated by deducting the control group from the experimental group. The mineralization ratio of aminopyrine was analyzed by a total organic carbon analyzer (TOC, multi N/C 2100S, Analytic Jena, Germany). Typically, the pH was adjusted to 2–3 by adding 25 μL HCl (2 M) to 5 mL samples. Pure N, Fe-CDs were used as the control, which was deducted from the TOC results of corresponding time.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, CA, DETA and FeCl3·6H2O were used as C, N and Fe sources, respectively. Under hydrothermal conditions, CA was first passivated by DETA to form amide bonds, which contributed to chelating Fe ions. Finally, after the process of polymerization and carbonization, the N and Fe co-doped carbon dots with sp2 graphitic structure were synthesized, on which there were abundant chemical groups, making them water-soluble.

The TEM of N, Fe-CDs exhibited a nearly spherical shape with an average particle size of 6.6 nm (Fig. 1b), which was slightly larger than that of N-CDs (6.1 nm) (Fig. S1a). From the HR-TEM image of N,Fe-CDs (Fig. 1c), the obvious lattice fringes with distances of 0.35 nm corresponded to the (002) plane of sp2 graphitic carbon [49]. Compared with the HR-TEM image of N-CDs (Fig. S1b), there were no visible particles or obvious iron oxide crystallite structure on the surface of N, Fe-CDs, suggesting that Fe in N, Fe-CDs mainly existed as the amorphous state rather than forming iron oxide crystal [50].

The chemical groups on the surface of CDs were examined by FT-IR. In the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs and N, Fe-CDs (Fig. S2), the bands at 3460 cm−1, 3150 cm−1 and 2975 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH, NH and CH, respectively [34,51]. The typical peaks at 1700 cm−1 and 1165 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO and CO, respectively [34]. The bands at 1400 cm−1 corresponded to aromatic CC/CN stretching vibration, confirming the framework of N doped sp2 graphitic carbon [41]. Compared with N-CDs, there was an extended peak at 622 cm−1 in N, Fe-CDs, corresponding to the FeO stretching vibration of amorphous iron-hydroxide [52,53]. In addition, two obvious peaks at 580 cm−1 and 820 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of FeN and FeOH, respectively, indicating that Fe was successfully introduced to CDs and mainly existed as Fe-N and Fe-OH complexes instead of iron oxide [42,52]. The above results indicated that there were rich in hydroxyl, amino, amide, etc. on the surface of N, Fe-CDs. Thus, N, Fe-CDs were of excellent water solubility and Fe in N, Fe-CDs could be well chelated and stabilized by these groups. As shown in the XRD pattern (Fig. S3), the broad peak appeared at 23.9°, corresponding to the amorphous SiO2 of the blank quartz substrate. There was only one sharp diffraction peak at 33.1° in the XRD pattern of N, Fe-CDs, indicating that Fe in N, Fe-CDs mainly existed as the amorphous state rather than forming the iron oxide crystal. In addition, the diffraction peak at 21.46° in N-CDs was in keeping with the (002) plane of sp2 graphitic carbon [49]. Compared with the graphitic carbon peak of N-CDs, there was a minor shift to 21.02° in N, Fe-CDs. Furthermore, the intensity of the graphitic peak in N, Fe-CDs was much lower than that of N-CDs, indicating that Fe was successfully doped to the sp2 graphitic structure [50].

The surface functional groups and element states in N, Fe-CDs were further investigated by XPS spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S4, the four peaks at around 286, 400, 532 and 710 eV in the full scan survey of N, Fe-CDs could be assigned to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Fe 2p, respectively. The elemental compositions of N-CDs and N, Fe CDs were shown in Table S2. Compared with N-CDs, the contents of C, O and N in N, Fe-CDs slightly declined due to the low doping rate of Fe (1.9%). As shown in the high-resolution C 1s spectra of N, Fe-CDs (Fig. 2 a), the peaks at 284.80, 286.08 and 287.94 eV were attributed to CC/CC, CN/CO and CO, respectively [34,54]. In the spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 2b), two peaks at 399.94 and 401.45 eV were attributed to CN/CN and NH bonds, respectively, which further confirmed the framework of N doped sp2 graphitic carbon [55,56]. The O 1s of N, Fe-CDs revealed two peaks at 533.12 and 531.42 eV (Fig. 2c), owing to the COH/COC and CO bands, respectively [57]. Compared with N-CDs, the peaks of N 1s and O 1s in N, Fe-CDs all shifted to higher binding energy, which might be attributed to the presence of Fe-N and Fe-OH complexes [50]. Fe, as the central atom, could provide empty orbitals, while N and O atoms could provide lone pairs electrons to form FeN and FeOH bonds, which led to a decrease in the electron density of the N and O atoms [50,58]. In the Fe 2p spectra of N, Fe-CDs (Fig. 2d), the Fe existed in four types: 709.78 eV for Fe2+ 2p3/2, 714.32 eV for Fe3+ 2p3/2, 723.17 eV for Fe2+ 2p1/2, 728.12 eV for Fe3+ 2p1/2 [59]. The co-existence of Fe3+ and Fe2+ suggested that Fe3+ could be reduced to Fe2+ in N, Fe-CDs, thus solving the problem of Fe(II) regeneration difficulty.

It was observed in Fig. S5a that there were two characteristic absorptions at ∼ 240 and ∼ 350 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra of N, Fe-CDs, owing to the π-π* transition of sp2 graphitic carbon and n-π* transition of CO/CN bands, respectively [34,60]. In comparison with N-CDs, there was a small shift of n-π* transition peak in N, Fe-CDs due to the formation of FeN and FeOH coordination bonds [61]. The FeN and FeOH complexes could generate light-induced light-induced ligand-to-mental charge transfer (LMCT) transition and enhance the absorption in UV–vis light region [61,62], which was demonstrated by the following decreased PL intensity and lifetime of N, Fe-CDs. As shown in Fig. S5b and Fig. S6, under the excitation wavelengths varying from 325 nm to 415 nm, the emission wavelengths of N, Fe-CDs showed redshifts from ∼ 410 nm to ∼ 480 nm due to the multiple mid-band energy states, which can be regulated by the particle size and surface chemical groups/surface trapping states of N, Fe-CDs [63]. In addition, N, Fe-CDs and N-CDs performed the highest blue PL intensity (λem = 445 nm) under excitation at 365 nm. As shown in Fig. S5c, N, Fe-CDs exhibited a lower PL intensity than that of N-CDs owing to the Fe-N and Fe-OH complexes between the chemical groups and Fe ions in N, Fe-CDs. Furthermore, the corresponding PL lifetime of N, Fe-CDs decreased from 11.6 ns to 8.3 ns (Fig. S5d), indicating the rapid PL quenching and highly efficient electron transfer properties in N, Fe-CDs [61,64].

The effects of pH, N, Fe-CDs dosage, PMS concentration, and aminopyrine concentration on aminopyrine photodegradation were investigated in Fig. 3 . As the pH increased from 3 to 9 under the conditions of 0.10 g/L N, Fe-CDs and 0.10 mM PMS, the corresponding photodegradation efficiencies of 0.10 mM aminopyrine gradually decreased from 99.9% to 37.4% in 60 min (Fig. 3a). The reason can be that different pH will influence the decomposition efficiency of PMS itself and Fe ions will gradually transform to Fe(OH)3 precipitation under pH＞3, which is not conducive to the activation of PMS [1,65]. Since the chelation effect between the functional groups and Fe ions in N, Fe-CDs has greatly improved the instability of Fe ions, the initial pH 4 performed almost the same photocatalytic activity as pH 3. The effect of N, Fe-CDs dosage was shown in Fig. 3b, as the concentration of N, Fe-CDs increased from 0.06 g/L to 0.10 g/L at constant PMS (0.10 mM), the corresponding photodegradation efficiencies of 0.10 mM aminopyrine gradually improved. However, as the N, Fe-CDs concentration was up to 0.12 g/L, there was no significant increase in the photodegradation efficiency of aminopyrine. The effect of PMS amount has also been investigated in the presence of 0.10 g/L N, Fe-CDs (Fig. 3c). In the tested range from 0.06 mM to 0.12 mM, the photocatalytic removal efficiency of 0.10 mM aminopyrine reached a high level (almost 100% in 60 min) when PMS amounts were 0.10 mM and 0.12 mM. The effect of aminopyrine concentration was shown in Fig. 3d. the photodegradation efficiencies almost reached 100% within 60 min (0.10 g/L N, Fe-CDs, 0.10 mM PMS) at aminopyrine concentration of less than 0.10 mM. Especially when aminopyrine concentration is 0.05 mM, it was almost completely degraded in 15 min. But when the aminopyrine concentrations were over 0.10 mM, the photocatalytic activity dramatically declined. Taking cost and high activities into consideration, 0.10 g/L N, Fe-CDs, 0.10 mM PMS, natural pH (4) and 0.10 mM aminopyrine were used for the following experiment.

In practical applications, anions including Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, HCO3 − and PO4 3−, might play an important role in reacting with ROS and activating PMS [66,67]. The anion experiments were studied under the above conditions (Fig. S7). There was a slight influence on the photocatalytic activity after adding Cl− and SO4 2−. However, with the introduction of NO3 −, HCO3 − and PO4 3−, the aminopyrine photodegradation rates were strongly inhibited and efficiencies significantly decreased from 99.0% to 82.6%, 54.9%, and 35.0%, respectively. The reason may be that NO3 −, HCO3 − and PO4 3− as scavengers will consume OH and SO4
−, and HCO3 − and PO4 3− can also alter the solution pH and precipitate Fe ions [67].

The photocatalytic removal efficiency of aminopyrine in N, Fe-CDs/PMS system was compared with those in N-CDs, PMS, Fe/PMS, N-CDs/PMS, N, Fe-CDs and N, Fe-CDs/PMS systems under visible light irradiation for 60 min. The results (Fig. 4 a) showed that the photocatalytic removal efficiency of aminopyrine in N, Fe-CDs/PMS system reached 99.0%, which was over 2-fold higher than those in other systems, demonstrating that N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity. The stability and recycling experiments of N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system were also carried out. As shown in Fig. 4b, the aminopyrine photodegradation percentage changed from 99.0% to 85.4% after recycling 5 times, demonstrating that the N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system possessed outstanding stability and recyclability.

The mineralization efficiency, as an important index to evaluate catalyst performance, was also detected by the TOC analyzer. As shown in Fig. 4c, under visible light irradiation for 120 min, the TOC removal efficiencies of 0.10 mM and 0.05 mM aminopyrine reached 33.8% and 63.4%, respectively. It was reported that free chlorine could only convert aminopyrine to some byproducts, but not mineralize it (Table S3) [45,47]. Compared with the traditional disinfectant treatments, the N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system exhibited an efficient mineralization performance. In addition, the mineralization efficiency of another antipyretic drug ibuprofen was also tested using N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system. Fig. S8 showed that the TOC removal percentage of 0.05 mM ibuprofen reached 81.3% in 120 min using the above system, indicating the favorable applicability of N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system on the removal of these contaminants.

As shown in Fig. 4d, under visible light irradiation, the cumulative photogeneration of Fe2+ gradually increased without PMS, indicating that Fe3+ could be effectively reduced to Fe2+ in N, Fe-CDs under visible light irradiation. While the photogenerated Fe2+ in the presence of PMS significantly decreased, which could be attributed to the Fe2+ consumption for the activation of PMS (Fe2+ + HSO5 − → SO4
− + Fe3+ + OH−) [7].

Herein, the photodegradation pathways of aminopyrine were analyzed by TOF LC-MS and GC–MS. The formed main degradation products in Table S4 and Table S5 indicated that the generated ROS could attack aminopyrine with high electron density. The photodegradation pathways included pyrazole ring cleavage, demethylation, hydroxylation, decarbonylation and benzene ring cleavage (Fig. 5 ), according to the MS information and a previous study [46]. The ring cleavage reaction, as the main pathway, occurred at the CC bond of pyrazole, which was oxidized to carbonyl (P1). P1 under the effects of demethylation and aldehyde loss would produce P2 and P3, respectively. Further demethylation of P2 produced P4, which was subsequently decarbonylated to form P5. Furthermore, P5 could further transform to P12 through hydroxylation according to a previous report [46]. CDs, as acid photocatalysts, have been shown to catalyze esterification reactions [68]. The presence of P13 indicated that P12 could be transformed to alcohol and carboxylic acid via the cleavage of benzene ring, which could be further mineralized to CO2 and H2O. Through the above degradation pathways, the molecular weight of aminopyrine gradually decreased and aminopyrine was finally mineralized to CO2 and H2O.

Active species trapping experiments were conducted to study the photocatalytic mechanism. Diverse scavengers including TBA for OH, MeOH for OH and SO4
−, L-histidine for 1O2, and BQ for O2 −, were employed in the reaction [66,69–71]. In the present study, our results showed that, after adding TBA and MeOH, the photodegradation efficiencies in 60 min decreased from 99.0% to 92.2% and 89.4% with the photocatalytic inhibition rates of 6.9% and 9.7%, respectively, demonstrating the presence of OH and SO4
− (Fig. 6 a and Fig. S9). Furthermore, there were more OH generation than SO4
−. After the introduction of L-histidine and BQ, it could be seen that the photodegradation efficiencies were significantly suppressed from 99.0% to 39.7% and 8.9%, respectively under visible light irradiation for 60 min, indicating that O2 − and 1O2 acted as the main ROS in the whole photocatalytic process.

ESR tests were conducted to further analyze the ROS. As shown in Fig. 6b, the typical peaks with a 1:2:2:1 intensity ratio and the smaller shoulder peaks suggested the existence of OH and SO4
− and proved more OH generation than SO4
− [66]. Fig. 6c showed the significant characteristic signal peaks of O2 −. In addition, a notable EPR signal in terms of 1:1:1 shown in Fig. 6d indicated the presence of 1O2 [66]. It was worth noting that signal intensities of O2 − and 1O2 were much higher than those of OH and SO4
−, further demonstrating the primary role of O2 − and 1O2 in the N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system.

As shown in Fig. S10a, the photocurrent response intensity of N, Fe-CDs was almost two folds than that of N-CDs, suggesting the more rapid photogenerated electron transfer properties in N, Fe-CDs. Moreover, Nyquist impedance plots were obtained through EIS experiments (Fig. S10b), in which the hemicycles diameter in the high-frequency region could provide the information of charge transfer resistance (Rct) analyzed by Z-view software. Compared with the Nyquist impedance plot of N-CDs (Rct = 4245 Ω), N, Fe-CDs (Rct = 1224 Ω) exhibited a smaller hemicycle and had almost 3.5-fold lower Rct, indicating the superior electronic conductivity and charge transfer ability of N, Fe-CDs [31,61,66]. This result was attributed to the introduction of Fe species. In N, Fe-CDs, Fe mainly existed in Fe-N and Fe-OH forms, which play an important role in harvesting visible light, enhancing the stability of Fe ions and acting as active sites to accelerate electron transfer. It has been shown that Fe chelates could boost Photo-Fenton processes [72,73]. Due to the light-induced LMCT of Fe-N and Fe-OH complexes, N, Fe-CDs exhibited better visible light absorption than N-CDs. Under visible light irradiation, N, Fe-CDs with sp2 graphitic structure could transform photon energy into electron energy, which was then transferred from ligands to Fe (III) through the LMCT process, thus accelerating Fe(II) regeneration to activate PMS for aminopyrine degradation. Combined with the above analysis, a photocatalytic mechanism of N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis was proposed (Fig. 7 ). Under visible light irradiation, the N doped sp2 graphitic structure was excited and generated photoinduced electrons. Then the photogenerated electrons could be directly transferred to Fe (III) to form Fe (II) via the LMCT process, which efficiently solved the problem of Fe (II) regeneration difficulty. The Fe (II) could activate PMS (HSO5 −) to produce SO4
− with the oxidation of Fe (II) to Fe (III), and SO4
− could further react with H2O to generate OH [1]. Meanwhile, Fe (II) could be oxidated to Fe (III) under the conversion of O2 to O2 − [74]. On the other hand, the photoinduced electrons could convert O2 to O2 −, which could be subsequently transformed to 1O2 under the effect of H2O [75]. Moreover, PMS might transfer electrons to Fe (III) and form SO5 ¯, thus accelerating the self-decomposition of PMS and forming 1O2 [4]. The generated ROS would finally degrade and mineralize aminopyrine to CO2 and H2O.

N, Fe-CDs possess a large specific surface area due to their zero-dimensional structure, which facilitates the removal of aminopyrine in aqueous solutions. However, due to the excellent hydrophilic property of N, Fe-CDs during practical application, to avoid their discharge, it is suggested that they may be immobilized on carriers including polymer membranes (e.g., polyacrylonitrile, polyethylene) [76]. Although Fe content in N, Fe-CDs was low, the release of Fe ions also needs to be considered during long usage. Thus, a comprehensive investigation of their management will be carried out to realize the transition from laboratory to engineering application.

Pyrazolone pharmaceutical aminopyrine could be efficiently degraded using N, Fe-CDs/PMS/vis system. During this process, N, Fe-CDs, as a visible light driver, can directly promote Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle for PMS activation. The characterization results found that Fe in N, Fe-CDs mainly existed as Fe-N and Fe-OH complexes, which could stabilize Fe ions and accelerate electron transfer. Under visible light irradiation, N, Fe-CDs (0.10 g/L) with only 1.9% Fe via activating PMS (0.10 mM) could remove almost 100% of aminopyrine (0.05 mM) in 15 min at natural pH 4. Moreover, Its TOC removal efficiency reached 63.4%. This result was attributed to the light-induced LMCT of Fe-N and Fe-OH complexes in N, Fe-CDs. O2 − and 1O2 acted as the main ROS in this whole process. These studies indicated that N, Fe-CDs with low cost and environmental friendliness was of potential application for PMS activation to remove wastewater containing pyrazolone pharmaceuticals.
